forum_threads.title: Does string tension matter as much in padel?
forum_threads.subtitle: Coming from tennis and a bit confused
forum_threads.content: In tennis I was obsessed with string tension. In padel, most rackets are foam-based. How much should I care about string tension and customization?

forum_posts.content: I see a lot of questions from new players about which racket to buy, so I thought I’d start a megathread. Please share: 1) your first padel racket, 2) what you liked/disliked, and 3) what level you were when you used it.
I started with a round, control-focused racket around 350g. Super forgiving on off-center hits and perfect for level 2. Avoid very head-heavy power rackets at the beginning.
Biggest tip for beginners: choose a soft, round racket with a large sweet spot. Don’t overthink brands—comfort and control matter more than power.
If your budget is tight, look for last year’s models. Many shops discount them heavily and they’re still perfect for beginners.
Try to demo at least two rackets if you can. A 10-minute hit is often enough to feel if the racket is too heavy or hurts your arm.
For absolute beginners I recommend staying under 360g and using a wrist strap. It reduces fatigue and you’ll control the ball better.
I made the mistake of buying a diamond-shaped power racket at level 2. Looked cool, but my errors went through the roof. Switched to a round control frame and improved a lot.
Weight is important, but balance is too. A slightly head-light racket usually feels easier to maneuver for new players.
If you have elbow or shoulder issues, prioritize softer foam and lower weight over everything else. Power can come later from technique.
In Madrid, several shops let you rent a beginner racket for a month and then buy it with a discount if you like it—great way to test before buying.
Thanks everyone for the recommendations. I went with a 355g round racket and feel much more confident at the net now.
For beginners who mostly defend, look for rackets marketed as ‘control’ or ‘all-round’. Avoid ‘hyper power’ marketing terms at first.
One more tip: overgrip thickness changes how heavy the racket feels. If it feels too heavy, try a slightly thinner grip.
I’m level 2 and my coach says soft foam + round shape helps me clear the net without swinging too hard, which protects my shoulder.
Another beginner here: buying second-hand is fine as long as there are no cracks. Small paint chips are okay.
I’m also looking for a light racket with good control and these comments have been super helpful, thank you all!
I’ve had some elbow issues and just started padel at level 2. What are your favorite light, arm-friendly rackets for beginners?
Look for rackets around 350g with soft foam. Some brands even advertise ‘comfort’ or ‘arm friendly’ in the name.
Also consider a slightly thicker overgrip to reduce vibration in the wrist and elbow.
I switched from 370g to 355g and the difference was huge for my elbow pain.
Don’t forget to stretch your forearm and shoulder after playing. The racket helps, but recovery matters too.
I’m trying to get two friends into padel and want a gear bundle (rackets + balls) under €80. Any ideas on good value starter sets?
Some supermarkets sell ‘starter packs’ with 2 basic rackets and 3 balls around €60. Not amazing quality but fine for level 1–2.
Check the marketplace here too—used beginner rackets plus new balls can still fit under €80.
I’ve been playing indoors with running shoes and I’m sliding too much. Are padel-specific shoes really worth it?
Yes, the herringbone sole on padel/tennis shoes gives much better grip and stability than running shoes.
When the ball heads toward the back wall I panic. Any easy drills or rules of thumb so I stop running away from the wall?
A simple drill: throw the ball at the back wall yourself and let it bounce once, then hit a gentle forehand. Repeat 50 times each session.
Think ‘let it come to you’. Give the ball space, bend your knees, and make contact slightly in front of your body after the rebound.
I’m level 2–3 and my serve is inconsistent. Does anyone have a simple routine I can repeat every time I serve?
Try this: 1) stand sideways, 2) bounce the ball once, 3) aim to the side glass, 4) hit at 70% power. Repeat exactly the same rhythm each serve.
Just moved near Sol, Madrid and I’m level 2. Looking for beginner-friendly clubs and group classes in the evenings.
Central Padel Club is very beginner friendly and has lots of evening social matches. Madrid West Padel is also great if you don’t mind a short trip.
For group lessons at level 2, I’d recommend Carla or Marta at Central—they’re very good with beginners.
For those who play at both: which club do you prefer for after-work matches, Central Padel Club or Madrid West Padel?
Central is easier to reach by public transport, but West feels a bit more relaxed on Friday nights.
I finally played my first amateur doubles tournament last weekend. Nerves were high, but it was a great experience. Here’s what I learned…
Congrats! Biggest lesson from my first tournament was to bring extra water and snacks. Long waits between matches can be draining.
We’ve just deployed several improvements to match search and calendar. Let us know what you think and report any bugs here.
Loving the new time filters—much easier to find evening doubles between 18:00 and 21:00 now.
What’s everyone’s favorite sports movie or documentary to watch before a match?
I always rewatch “Rocky II” before tournament finals. Cheesy but motivating!
In tennis I obsessed over string tension. In padel, should I worry about it or just use the stock setup?
In padel the foam core matters more than strings, but string condition still affects feel. Just restring when they look worn or feel ‘dead’.
